data.title: Trump Pauses Greenland Tariffs - The 'Framework' Is The Real Risk
data.description:
Trump Backs Off Greenland Tariffs

The news came out just a little while ago. I had finished dinner and was unwinding and relaxing (it’s 8:30 PM here, and that means things are calm...as usual). My phone blew up with notifications from Bloomberg, then CNBC, then X, Seeking Alpha, etc., all saying the same thing—that Trump has suspended the tariffs that were supposed to go into effect on Feb 1 for the Greenland trade.

And right away, the mood changed: from “we’re at high risk” to “okay, let’s relax.”

Whether this is a legitimate agreement or simply a calculated tactical maneuver (in the best of deal-making tradition) will depend on the actual language and content of the "framework of the agreement" they claim to have on Greenland, and there will likely be disagreements and differing opinions on both sides. From a practical perspective, however, the fact that the tariffs scheduled to begin on February 1 will not go into effect does substantially reduce the immediate risk. The structural component of uncertainty continues to exist in the background (like the potential for rapid verbal escalation via social media), and Europe and other countries will need to continue to accept the risk of these events occurring as long as Trump is in office.

For now, at least, the markets should be able to get back to analyzing quarterly results and the state of the overall economy, but this whole process may provide a basis for a slightly higher risk premium for risky assets, since the likelihood of the pattern repeating itself is very high.

What We Know About the “Framework” (and What We Don’t)

A number of things remain unclear at the moment as follows: President Trump states he has "defined the framework for a future agreement" on Greenland and also "the entire Arctic region," after meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, and that it is "based upon this agreement" that he will not enforce the tariffs scheduled to go into effect on February 1st. As usual, Trump did not provide specifics on framework conditions or other key elements, while the Danish side's public line remains that sovereignty is non-negotiable and can’t be traded away. Additionally, we are all aware of how mercurial President Trump is, and while this current crisis may have been temporarily resolved, he can easily stir up more controversy and tension in the future regarding Greenland.

Additionally, within the same message, Trump slips in a little nugget that is hardly trivial: "Further talks" on the Golden Dome missile defense system "with regards to Greenland," and he has already begun placing people on the negotiating table for these talks (i.e., Vice President JD Vance, Marco Rubio, Steve Witkoff, etc.). To put it simply for everyone, we are not witnessing a formally signed treaty, but an informal "framework" that eliminates short-term pressure (tariffs) where everyone will try to get something, including him, just as he has always tried to do.

It is also reported that the U.S. wishes to become "involved" in Greenland's strategic rights/assets. This has not yet been confirmed; however, I would anticipate that a "negotiated" agreement will strengthen the U.S.'s role in Arctic security/early warning/missile defense and provide access to Greenland's natural resources, while Denmark retains official sovereignty over Greenland. As a reminder, Greenland is estimated to hold natural resource reserves valued at up to $5 trillion.

Davos Has Turned Into the "Greenland G7"

Theoretically, it should have been the usual Davos: growth, inflation, energy, AI, etc. In fact, before the big announcement, Greenland had sucked all the oxygen out of the room. As we all know, after all, there is no Davos, unless there is a crisis to solve, right?

Day 2

Yesterday, the first person to try to take center stage was French President Macron: tone of an EU leader, blue sunglasses, and an obvious message: "We can't let ourselves get blackmailed." Most importantly, a sense that Europe must act together. Of course, the same old European speeches I can confirm I've heard one thousand times. We need to create a single entity, a strong and unified body, etc. The problem is that there isn't enough unity among European states; we have seen that through how they have been debating the issue of the U.S. blocking certain Chinese companies. For some of them it is about trade, for other people it is about security, and for other people it is simply their own national politics masquerading as geopolitical issues. If the objectives aren't aligned, the red lines get stretched.

Day 3

Today, Trump acted as Trump does but had an extremely well-constructed timeline. He started out on the WEF stage establishing what he called the "strong America/weak Europe" paradigm with the "you're getting kept on your toes" tone, and with jabs at his allies, as always seems to be more apparent at Davos than when he speaks at rallies.

I thought it was great how he transitioned to the topic everyone wanted to hear, including myself: Greenland. He almost seemed reluctant to speak about it; however, that is also part of the rhetoric of a leader who is able to stand on the stage in front of millions of people watching him. And he jumped headfirst into the discussion, not with the language of classic geopolitics, but with that of a real estate negotiation: he presented it as a "small request" in comparison to what the U.S. will "gift" Europe for years to come. Within that context, he reiterated the primary goal ("rights, title, and property") while making it clear that he will use both economic and diplomatic leverage on allies and that he will not use military force ("I must not/will not").

The most memorable passage for me is that of an ultimatum in disguise. "If you agree, 'we will be grateful'; if you don't agree, 'we will remember.' This statement has really taken many people aback due to the way the language was used; it is no longer "Let's talk about negotiating" and now is simply "You choose, or there will be a price to pay." He interjected Ukraine into the negotiation process, and here, in my opinion, lies the filthiest (and therefore most relevant) part of the story: he called the war a "bloodbath," stated that Putin desires an agreement and Zelenskyy also desires an agreement, and went so far as to imply a relationship between the Ukraine and Greenland dossiers ("First peace... and then 'Now what I am asking for is a piece of ice'"). Cool! "Piece of Ice" is very cool!

Market Reaction

Firstly, the markets did not "buy" an agreement, but they "bought" the removal of tail risk over the very short term. The moment Trump said he would not impose the Feb 1st tariff, as he had agreed on a framework with the NATO Secretary General, Wall Street turned on the biggest and oldest lever there is: taking off hedges. Below are the major stock indices and S&P 500 (SPX) sectors at market closing time.

Treasuries did their part (10Y down to 4.25-4.26%), and the dollar stopped sliding, as well as the market volatility unwound.

Then take a look at the sectors, and the "IT and discretionary leading, and energy lower" story does not fit, since Energy is the largest sector of the day, up +2.4%, followed by Materials up +1.9%, while IT is up, but not the large leader +1.0%. Therefore, this is an example of a "pro-cycle/pro-risk" type of rotation (as opposed to the "in with growth/out with defense" type), which typically involves buying a small amount of every sector that was sold off heavily in the previous day's trading and then placing the best performing sectors (when tariffs are not a headline) at the top of your order flow. This is very interesting.

Then we have gold, the one that has my attention: Gold hasn't moved like the other asset classes as they've all bounced back. Gold has been going down since it reached its high for the day, although it was only by a small margin. A large shooting star was printed on the chart following a very aggressive squeeze, and this type of pattern doesn't appear on charts very frequently; therefore, this should be monitored closely because it is typically a signal from the market stating, "I got a little too hot in the short term (but my run may not be over)."

The steep trend line of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is now significantly lower than where gold is trading at currently; therefore, gold can easily fall another $250 and still trade near the 21-day moving average. The 50-day moving average is also significantly lower, approximately $400 under current levels. Even though the near-term risks seem to be decreasing today, the market is still keeping some form of insurance, just redistributing its weight.

For me, the market is not celebrating the completion of an agreement. Instead, the market is factoring in a pause (the removal of February 1st from the calendar) with sufficient hedge positions to indicate that the "framework" is still an empty box.

I'm Still Constructive, but I'm Not Relaxing

As far as the February 1 tariffs are concerned, they may be behind us for the time being; however, the "Greenland dossier" is still out there. The reason the markets rebounded today is due to the fact that today's actions reduced the near-term tail risk. However, today's action did nothing to reduce the structural risk, as the bargaining chip remains the same: Greenland, sovereignty, Arctic security, and now (possibly) mining rights.

In my opinion, the term "regulatory framework" is a time-buyer. It cools down the rhetoric. It allows everyone to present their own national-level narratives ("we have de-escalated", "we have started negotiations," "we have preserved our sovereignty") -- but, it also introduces an enormous amount of uncertainty into the marketplace because it establishes a type of box that can be filled with whatever someone wants. For negotiation purposes, this is ideal -- but for the purposes of assessing risk, it is disastrous, as one cannot determine what type of "filling" will occur (symbolic -- greater coordination on Arctic defense; financial -- economic concessions; or strategic -- access to resources/mining).

That is the reason I do not think this event is "over" -- although it is "on hold."

From a practical viewpoint, I will continue to be constructive in my viewpoint and continue to purchase stocks for two reasons. First, the underlying fundamentals that are driving the stock market have not changed. Second, earnings season has driven the stock market for many years, and it will likely continue to do so. Liquidity will also continue to be an important factor for many years to come; however, with the removal of calendar risk, positions can quickly move from contraction to expansion.​‍​‌‍​‍‌ I don't think today's rally signifies a return to normality, but I think some sort of tactical normalization is very likely. I'm adjusting to the market, and I think this is a pause in the conflict, but not a definitive peace.‌‍​‍‌

data.title: F.N.B. Corporation: A Well-Run Bank With Significant Excess Capital
data.description:
Shares of F.N.B. Corporation (FNB) have been a solid performer over the past year, rising 11%. Investors received further good news as the company reported strong Q4 results, sending shares up about 3% in response. The company has an excellent capital position, facilitating ongoing balance sheet growth and improving margins. These tailwinds led me to upgrade shares to a “Buy” in November, and that call has played out with shares gaining 14% since then. Shares have even rallied past my $17 price target, and so with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit FNB.

In the company’s fourth quarter, FNB earned $0.50 per share, blowing past estimates by $0.09. For the full year, the company earned $1.59, above my $1.50 expectation. Results were flattered by the realization of a net $33 million of tax credits due to a renewable project, partially offset by a voluntary $20 million donation to its charitable foundation. Overall, earnings were closer to $0.02 favorable on a true run-rate basis in my view. FNB delivered a solid quarter, and its excellent capital position gives FNB significant capacity for growth and shareholder returns.

FNB Continues To Expand Its Balance Sheet

Deposits increased by $700 million from Q3, and they were up a solid 4.5% from last year, consistent with a strong track record of deposit growth. Noninterest-bearing (“NIB”) balances were 25.6% of total deposits, and this metric has now been stable for nearly two years; I expect that a bottom has been reached, which will support margins going forward. Deposit costs fell 47bps from a year ago to 2.53%. So far, this cutting cycle, FNB has passed on 37% of rate cuts, a bit less than the 40% of rate hikes it passed on. 94% of its $7.3 billion in time deposits mature over the next year, and I expect them to reprice lower, which should put some downward pressure on deposit costs.

Average loans increased by 1.9% sequentially to $35 billion; they were also up 3.4% from last year. The company also moved $200 million of lower-yielding residential mortgages to “held for sale” as it prepares to sell them in Q1 to reallocate capital to higher-yielding loans. FNB has a 90% loan-to-deposit ratio, a level I would like to see FNB sustain, meaning loan growth and deposit growth should be fairly closely correlated going forward.

As you can see below, its loan portfolio has a 25% allocation to commercial real estate (“CRE”). It also has a 25% allocation to residential mortgages, which is outsized in my view, given FNB’s strong capital position. As noted above, FNB is selling down some of its allocation, and I expect it to focus loan growth towards business lending. While this allocation is low risk, gradually reorienting the loan portfolio elsewhere should enhance margins and EPS over time.

CRE continues to be a sector garnering significant investor scrutiny, but FNB’s portfolio is solid. First, I would note it is a granular portfolio with 42% of balances below $5 million, limiting the impact of any one loan going bad. Just 18% of the CRE portfolio is office, and even here, delinquencies are just 1.29%. While this is higher than its total portfolio, many peers have 5+% delinquency rates in this asset class, speaking to FNB’s conservative underwriting. 27% of the portfolio is multifamily, and while rents face some pressure, I do not see this being a credit issue ultimately. I am comfortable with FNB’s loan portfolio.

FNB generated a 3.28% net interest margin (“NIM”) in Q4. This was up 3bps from Q3 and 24bps from last year, thanks to benefits from fixed reinvestment and deposit rate cuts. FNB has $1.2 billion of cash flow from its investment portfolio in 2026 at a yield of 3.06%. FNB should be able to reinvest proceeds above 4%, providing a $12-15 million run-rate benefit to net interest income by year-end.

Fee revenue was up 9% from last year to $92 million, led by strong client trading activity, which boosted securities commissions by 31%. Trust and service charges were also strong. I would note that FNB generates about $50 million/year in card interchange fees, which could face downward pressure if Congress passes legislation to reduce them. I view this as no more than a $10 million risk, or about $0.02/share, which is quite manageable and would not be sufficiently material to alter my investment thesis in FNB.

Credit Quality And Capital Are Strong

Credit quality remains solid. Net charge-offs were 19bps, down from 24bps a year ago. FNB has 31bps of nonperforming loans, down 6bps from Q3 and 17bps from a year ago. During the quarter, FNB set aside $19 million in provisions for losses. Allowances now cover 418% of NPLs, up from 265% a year ago. I view coverage above 250% as strong. This strong level of reserves gives FNB a cushion should NPLs increase, and the company is unlikely to need to add to reserves even in a modest economic downturn. If anything, should credit conditions remain stable, there is scope for modest reserve reduction over the next year.

FNB remains extremely well capitalized, with a record tier 1 common equity ratio (CET1) of 11.4%. Capital is up 80bps from a year ago. Even factoring in all unrealized losses on its securities' portfolio, capital would be 10.9%, far above historic norms. FNB does not need more than 10% capital to safely run its business, so it has scope to grow the asset base about 10% without increasing capital at all. As a result, it can both grow its balance sheet and return most of its earnings to shareholders, gradually working down its excess capital position. This excess capital could also provide flexibility to do bolt-on M&A should opportunities arise.

Shares offer a 2.8% dividend yield, which I view as highly secure given its capital position. FNB repurchased $18 million of stock in Q4. Due to modest buybacks, its share count is down about 0.6% from last year. I expect a somewhat faster pace of buybacks in 2026 with a capital return yield that is likely to be closer to 5%. FNB has not increased its dividend since the great financial crisis, and I view higher buybacks as more likely than a dividend increase.

FNB Is Attractive With Upside Past $20

FNB also provided guidance for 2026, which I view as broadly credible. The company is targeting mid-single-digit balance sheet growth, similar to 2025. If anything, I see scope for some loan growth acceleration relative to 2025, given less macroeconomic uncertainty. I also anticipate two rate cuts this year, and I expect provisions to be in the lower half of guidance given FNB’s strong reserve coverage currently.

Back in November, I was targeting $1.65 in 2026 EPS. I am now targeting $1.65-$1.75 in 2026 EPS, reflecting the continued strength in credit quality and slightly later Fed rate cut timing. Tangible book value is $11.87, and FNB has about $1.20 of excess capital, though I expect FNB to use this excess capital to fund growth rather than lead to outsized shareholder returns. Given this capital position and my view that a recession is unlikely, I do think FNB’s outperformance can continue. Fundamentally, it should trade closer to the 12x P/E of other regional banks, pointing to a ~$20.50 fair value. As such, I remain a buyer and see FNB poised for continued outperformance in 2026.

data.title: Ally Financial Takes A More Aggressive Capital Allocation Approach (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Ally Financial Inc. (ALLY) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 11%. That said, shares have pulled back about 10% from their highs, and a Q4 earnings beat on Wednesday morning did little to lift shares. While credit quality has been resilient, capital has been a bit lower than I like to see, and Ally is set to be more aggressive in returning capital than I anticipated. I last covered shares in November, upgrading Ally to a “Buy,” and shares have returned 10% since then, surpassing and then retracting to my $43 price target. With updated financials and 2026 guidance in hand, now is a good time to revisit ALLY.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Ally earned $1.09, which beat estimates by $0.07 as revenue grew 3% to $2.2 billion. Over the past year, Ally has refocused its business on its core auto-lending operation, selling off non-core assets like its credit card unit, and that renewed focus is paying off in improving margins and returns while enhancing the capital efficiency of its balance sheet. Ally reported a core return on tangible equity of 11.1%, up from 9.3% last year, but still relatively low given low-yielding fixed assets and elevated credit costs. These headwinds should gradually fade.

Interest Margins Continue To Improve

Ally originated $10.8 billion of consumer auto loans and a 9.62% yield. 42% of loans went to borrowers in its highest credit tier, but the average FICO score did tick down to 706 from 720 last year. While still prime, we may be seeing Ally slowly expand its credit standards after years of tightening. The portfolio currently yields 9.32%, so these originations will help to further lift the overall portfolio yield. This origination benefit, alongside falling deposit costs due to Fed rate cuts, is helping to widen margins.

Ally’s net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded 18bps from last year to 3.48%. Net financing revenue was up $91 million to $1.6 billion. The company’s yield on interest-earning assets fell by 35bps to 6.87%. This was primarily due to the sale of its high-yielding credit card business and lower yields on commercial auto loans, offset by the retail auto tailwind noted above. Against this, funding costs fell an even sharper 56bps to 3.69%, driving the improvement in margins.

Ally had $149 billion of deposits, down from $151.5 billion last year. To a large extent, this decline in deposits is voluntary. With Ally limiting the size of its balance sheet given its capital position, it does not need to grow deposits and can instead more aggressively price them to maximize margins, allowing some attrition. In fact, deposit costs were down 63bps over the past year, reflecting over 80% of the decline in the Fed funds rate. This is a very strong pass-through as time deposits have matured and repriced lower. Frankly, the relative stability of the deposit base amidst such a pronounced repricing is encouraging. I expect deposit costs to drift a bit lower as time deposits mature, but the beta to rate cuts will likely be lower in 2026 as we see the balance sheet likely return to growth by midyear.

Credit Quality Has Normalized

During the quarter, Ally set aside $487 million for credit losses, down $70 million from last year. This was $35 million more than its $452 million of net charge-offs. Auto-net charge-offs were 2.14%, down 20bps from a year ago. Net charge-offs typically rise seasonally over the course of the year, so the sequential rise is not particularly concerning to me, as it is consistent with normal trends. I expect an improvement in Q1, especially given tax refunds (which should be larger than in prior years).

Against this, Ally has set aside $3.2 billion for potential auto credit losses. The company has kept its share of coverage constant at 3.7% for four straight quarters. This is 41bp above its “normal” level to reflect the fact that charge-offs have been elevated. This level of reserves provides about 2x coverage of charge-offs. Absent a recession, I view this as sufficient coverage, though a downturn in economic activity would likely lead Ally to set aside additional reserves.

Adjusted Capital Is Low

Ally has a 10.2% tier 1 common equity ratio (CET1), which appears strong; however, when fully phasing in future regulatory changes, its capital ratio would only be 8.3%, which is uncomfortably low, especially as Ally’s loan book carries higher credit risk than most banks. Given its comparatively low capital ratio, ALLY has closely managed risk-weighted assets, selling off high-risk businesses like its credit card business and issuing credit-risk transfers to reduce the capital intensity of its auto loans. I view 9.5% as an appropriate normalized capital ratio, and I have expected that Ally would wait for adjusted capital to reach 9% before buying back stock, which will likely take ~12-18 months depending on loan growth. It appears management is likely to move earlier. This will further delay a return to 9+% adjusted capital.

In fact, Ally has authorized a $2 billion share repurchase program. I would note that this program is open-ended and may take multiple years to be completed; there is no requirement to buy shares. Management said it will move “low and slow” with $24 million in repurchases so far and that the pace of buybacks will pick up after adjusted CET1 hits 9%. I would expect 2026 buybacks to be $400-$600 million under this approach, and if we saw the economy turn down (not my base case), this number could be undershot. Shares also offer a 2.8% dividend yield, which I view as secure given Ally’s underlying earnings power.

Looking at 2026 guidance, management is targeting a 3.6%-3.7% NIM assuming two rate cuts (which is consistent with my own expectation). Ally also expects 1.8%-2% of auto charge-offs, which is approaching its long-term target after facing a period of excess credit losses. It is notable that charge-offs are coming down even as unemployment is likely to be higher this year than last year. This reflects the tightening of Ally’s underwriting standards over the past two years, which has improved the overall quality of its loan book and is starting to exert more notable downward pressure on credit costs. I expect charge-offs to be in the upper half of this range.

Capital Policy Pushes Me To A "Hold"

Back in November, I saw Ally earning at least $4.50 per share in 2026, though I did not expect buybacks before 2027. Given economic uncertainty and an unemployment rate that is higher than a year ago, I would rather have seen ALLY hold off on buybacks and further build its capital position, though I recognize this is a more conservative stance. I view buybacks as an aggressive capital allocation choice but not a wholly unreasonable one. With buybacks and the company looking to grow its balance sheet more quickly than I previously assumed, I now see ALLY earning $4.60-$5.05 this year on its $40.38 tangible book value.

At ~$43, shares are trading about 9x earnings and 1.05x tangible book value. This is not an expensive stock; however, it is now taking a more aggressive stance to its capital position, which, in my view, leaves shares exposed if there is a rolling over in the economy. While not my base case, with unemployment having risen, this is not an impossibility. As a result, after their 10% run in just about two months, I am moving shares to “Hold.” Valuation is cheap enough that a “Sell” is unmerited, but its capital allocation leaves less margin for error in a cyclical business, and so I would recommend looking elsewhere for better risk/reward opportunities.

data.title: Is Greenland 2026 The Next 'Liberation Day'? International Investment Ideas
data.description:
Davos is hotter than usual this time of year

The market (SPY, QQQ, IVV, DIA, VOO) is closely hanging on every word or gaffe President Trump delivers at Davos. The President arrived at the Switzerland Enclave for the global institutional elite and government leaders on the 21st of January. In anticipation of this event, Trump preempted his arrival by casting a myriad of possibilities on how the U.S. under Trump's administration planned to take control of Greenland, which is currently ruled by Denmark. Proposals included an outright purchase and or possible occupation if that doesn't work out. The market didn't like it:

The major U.S. indexes promptly gave back most of the gains for the year, with the more conservative Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) still hanging on to a positive total return. Upon arrival, Trump changed his tone a bit, claiming that the U.S. wouldn't use force to acquire the territory. As we all know at this point, the maelstrom of possible narrative shifts in the days to come could be numerous.

Investors still have PTSD from the tariff tantrum flash crash of 2025 that was "Liberation Day." With more tariffs being volleyed by both sides, here is a summary of what has been proposed up until now:

  • A 10% tariff on European Exports to start Feb 1st on countries that opposed the Greenland acquisition.- now rescinded
  • An increase to a 25% tariff if no agreement is to be had by June 1st, 2026.
  • A $108 goods value target for tariffs against various U.S. imports to the EU by the EU.
  • A possible cancellation of the EU/U.S. trade deal that has yet to be passed by the EU.

So what gives, and why is Greenland so important?

The story of Fallout

As an older millennial who grew up on various popular PC and console video games, the story of the Fallout series seems applicable. There is also currently a popular show on Amazon Prime about the series. The story itself starts in a pre-war era [pre 2077]. Corporations more or less take over as the world becomes more capitalistic and resources become more scarce. The series then had a resource war around 2050 where world powers began to acquire and invade new territories in search of energy-related commodities. This all culminates in a massive 2077 war, devastating the world as we know it.

Sound eerily similar? Well, humans are logical predictors of their own destinies; we all hope to avoid massive wars in the future, and everyone gets along, but AI has set off a resource hunt. While the resources may not be needed right now, I have an inkling that tech giants [corporations] have forewarned just how short the world will be on resources once AI exponentially takes off and consumes more and more power. Many tech CEOs have already stated that, at this point, the only limitations to AI are basically energy and commodity-related. Robots included here.

Greenland, to its benefit or detriment, is resource-rich and untapped.

Camp Century, Cold War era vs. now

Another popular topic circulating regarding Greenland has to do with U.S. military installations. First off, this is not the only time the U.S. has tried to purchase Greenland; here are the others:

  • In 1867, after the U.S. Alaska purchase.
  • 1910 under President Taft.
  • 1946 under President Truman.

The US, in addition to these purchase attempts, had much more freedom over what it could do in Greenland during the Cold War era. The most notable of which was the construction of Camp Century. This fully documented [even via video documentary all over YouTube] was a sub-ice lattice work base powered by modular nuclear reactors [yes, we had those even in the 1960s]. Being as close to the Soviet Union in a first strike scenario was of utmost importance to the U.S. military at this time, which also feared Russia's first Sputnik satellite, which they believed could be weaponized.

The details are numerous and beyond the scope of this article. The large site was abandoned after the shifting ice deteriorated the roof structures. Along with the abandonment was hazardous nuclear waste from the modular reactors. The ice is now melting, and local Greenlanders fear the environmental repercussions of that 1960s-era installation.

Strong local governance

While the U.S. has a coastal military installation at Thule, it is unlikely that the U.S. would ever be able to build a subterranean base again. In the Cold War era, Denmark ruled from afar, and locally, there was little governance. Now Greenland has its own parliament to manage internal affairs. Environmental issues are of strong importance. Quickly strip mining the territory for valuable resources or installing more modular reactors to populate remote areas are probably off the table now in this regard.

International dividend funds

So where does that leave us? This story is sure to be on again, off again. If the investor believes there will be exiting of U.S. equities and debt, particularly by European institutions, here are some ideas.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY) and The Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYMI). I've previously covered both of these high-yield value ex-U.S. dividend ETFs. My main thesis was not only an International rotation after a long U.S. bull run, but the depreciation of the U.S. dollar, which was also at a high point versus foreign currencies. Currency return is when you are buying foreign assets in dollars [such as equities], and the share prices listed in foreign territories and foreign currencies appreciate versus your dollar.

You get a currency return in addition to any capital appreciation. The same goes for dividends being passed on to the investor. From a planning perspective, currency return investments of foreign assets are ideal when you think your own currency has reached its peak valuation or parity to other currencies.

The past 12 months have seen both funds have an S&P 500 (SP500) beating total return. They still remain at much lower average P/E ratios than the S&P 500 as well.

ETF P/E Source
SCHY 15.4 From Schwab
VYMI 13.51 From Vanguard

Even after the big run up, both have attractive dividend yields in excess of 3.5%

If you'd like to view my previous write ups on these ETFs you can find them here: SCHD + SCHY: Chaos & Layoffs, An Easy Rate And Currency Hedge; VYMI: International Dividend ETF For Dollar Deprecation.

International blend

For someone just seeking an international blend exposure, not income oriented, The Vanguard Total International Stock Index Fund ETF Shares (VXUS) is a good starter fund. This large cap fund currently has a P/E ratio of only 17.1 X:

Again, like the value funds, this large cap non-U.S. blend fund trades at a much lower earnings multiple than the S&P 500.

This low P/E ratio is again impressive after a large run up over the past 12 months.

Holdings

This is about as diverse an ETF as you'll find, with 8,710 holdings. Taiwan Semiconductor sits atop but still only represents 2.9% of the fund. This ETF is currently very overweight in global financials.

International bonds

The Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (BNDX) is a good way to get a diverse exposure to international bonds. The threat of the EU dumping U.S. debt in retaliation could only mean they'd put more into their own sovereign debt. Although unlikely to happen in my opinion, the prospect of increased demand for ex-U.S. debt could mean appreciation in addition to yield. And that yield is also subject to currency return in the case of a dollar decline, which is also a plus.

This bond fund currently yields near the top of it's 10-year average.

The fund is overweight Europe, and can be seen as heavily exposed to EU sovereign debt.

The fund is 77.78% A credit rating and above with only a small portion in non-investment grade debt.

Final thoughts and summary

How good has ex-U.S. investing been over the last 12 months? Let's take a look at a 60/40 stocks/bonds portfolio against the S&P 500 over this time frame:

In this allocation I assigned 40% to BNDX and 20% to each of the equity funds mentioned.

This conservative international portfolio delivered a 21.13 % CAGR versus the S&P 500 with only a 3.08% standard deviation. The Sharpe Ratio [risk-adjusted return] was vastly skewed toward this portfolio during this duration of time [4.96 is very high for a risk adjusted return]. Can it keep up? Hard to say, but it's been a great 12 months for non-U.S. investments. Jeremey Grantham is probably sleeping very well at night.

I would expect the rhetoric on Greenland to keep up until there is either a reprieve in the Russia-Ukraine war, making the territory less of an immediate strategic point, or if a monetary deal gets inked. Even if this deal takes years to complete, it should placate Trump for a while.

data.title: Bond Volatility Is So Low, It's Concerning
data.description:
Why bond volatility matters?

In recent articles, I’ve explained why I am not particularly concerned about the record prices and/or valuation multiples of equity indices (such as the DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and small cap (RTY) in themselves. The reason is quite simple – equity prices and valuation multiples need to be contextualized by a range of other factors. As detailed in those articles, these factors include US corporate profitability metrics, potential for global market reach, risk-free rates, etc.

Under this broader context, today I want to examine a factor that has entered my radar recently: bond volatility. As will be detailed later, bond volatility is currently hovering near a multi-year low. From experiences, such muted volatility is unlikely to last and has historically preceded significant equity market shifts. As such, the main thesis of this article is twofold. First, I want to identify the top reasons that could cause bond volatility to increase in the near term. And second, I want to explore the implications of such an increase to SP500 returns.

Let me start by showing how low the bond volatility currently is for readers not following this parameter. Take TLT ETF as an example (a proxy for long-term treasury bonds). Its implied volatility (IV) is only 11.8, as shown in the first chart below. This is in the 29% percentile rank, which means that TLT’s IV was only lower than the current level 29% of the time in the last year. When corporate bonds and bonds with shorter durations are included, the picture is even more extreme. For example, the second chart below shows the 30-day rolling volatility of the BND ETF as a proxy for the aggregated bond market. As of this writing, volatility has decreased to 2.74% only, which sits significantly below the historical average of 5.68% and also approaches the multi-year low of 2.24% recorded in early 2021.

MOVE index and relevance to equity

Finally, let’s also examine the so-called MOVE index. The full name of the index is the ICE BofA Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate. It is the most important index for bond market volatility in my view, and I consider it the VIX index for bonds. Just like the VIX index gauges investors’ fear for the SP500, the MOVE Index serves as a fear gauge for the bond market.

To wit, as of this writing, the MOVE Index has compressed toward levels not seen since the relatively stable pre-pandemic era, as shown in the next chart. In my experience, a reading between 80 and 120 is normal, but the reading has dropped to only 66.6 currently. To me, this reflects severe market complacency and indicates the market’s underestimation of bond volatility.

As repeatedly argued in my other articles, the bond market and equity market are essentially the two sides of the same coin. As another reflection of this duality, the next chart illustrates the strong correlation between the S&P 500 and the BofAML MOVE Index. Note that the MOVE index is plotted on an inverted scale; thus, what this chart really shows is a strong negative correlation. As seen, the MOVE index has correlated closely – and again negatively – with the SP500 index both in the longer term (the chart is for a period of ~3 years) and also in the near term.

There are several fundamental forces behind such historical correlation, and I don’t see any changes in these forces under our current conditions. To start, bonds serve as the discount rate for all financial assets. As such, a rise in bond volatility inevitably leads to changes in the VIX and increases the "equity risk premium". Secondly and consequently, as the relative valuation dynamics between bonds and stocks change, investors – both individual and especially institutional investors – tend to start reallocating their exposure. Given the amount of funds in the bond pool, a relatively small allocation shift towards the bond market could create large selling pressure on equity. As to be detailed next, I do see large odds for this scenario in the near future.

Other risks and final thoughts

An immediate uncertainty facing the bond market is the potential of Europe to adjust their US exposure as the rhetoric between the U.S. and Europe over Greenland heats up. European investors hold a considerable amount of US bonds and could become less willing to hold onto them if the geopolitical risks escalate. More details of this development are quoted below from a recent Seeking Alpha News:

Europe, as America’s largest lender, holds considerable leverage—European countries own $8T in U.S. bonds and equities, nearly twice as much as the rest of the world combined. Deutsche Bank analysts suggest that as the geoeconomic stability of the Western alliance faces existential disruption, European investors may become less willing to maintain their current dollar exposure. Danish pension funds have already begun repatriating money and reducing their dollar holdings, a trend that could accelerate across the continent following recent developments.

As such, together with other uncertainties (such as the uncertainties surrounding the next Fed Chair), I do see good odds A) for bond market volatility to surge significantly in the near future and also B) for the equity market to be negatively impacted. Moreover, a sudden spike in bond volatility could have an especially negative impact on the S&P 500 under the current market conditions for a couple of reasons. First, US equity valuation is currently elevated and also heavily concentrated in high-growth technology sectors, which tend to make the valuation more sensitive to changes in the discount rates. Secondly, the volatility of the S&P 500 is also quite low, as shown in the next two charts, indicating complacency in the equity market too. SPY’s 30-day rolling volatility is only 10.1% currently, well below its historical average of 15.6%. The IV of SPY, despite the surge in the recent 1 or 2 days, still hovers close to its 52-week average, as seen in the second chart (16.7 vs. 15.4, a difference of about 8% only).

To close, my goal for this market outlook is triggered by the unusually low volatility in the bond market. Against this backdrop, my goals in this article are A) to explain why bond volatility could increase significantly in the near term and B) to explore the implications of such an increase for the SP500. I see significant downside risks in the near term and recommend hedging strategies. As one possible idea, investors could consider buying put options on the S&P 500 and/or bonds given their low volatilities (and thus favorable premiums for buyers).

data.title: Gold Reduces Drawdowns More Than Volatility
data.description:
Gold is often misunderstood as a volatility dampener. Though in reality, its primary benefit comes from the reduction of portfolio drawdowns, not volatility reduction. This distinction is important to understand because it matters for portfolio construction and investor behavior during periods of market stress.

Gold tends to exhibit periods of elevated volatility and can remain range-bound for extended periods of time. However, gold has historically

  • preserved portfolio liquidity
  • recovered faster than risk assets following systemic shocks
  • and outperformed during left-tail events

As a result, portfolios with exposure to gold often experience shallower peak-to-trough losses during broad market downturns.

Gold functions as a liquidity reserve during periods of market stress. Gold is a deep liquid market, which makes gold an asset that can be easily monetized during periods of market turmoil. Gold can be sold or pledged without reliance on a normally functioning credit market. During periods of forced deleveraging, which is a common feature at the onset of a market meltdown, gold often becomes a source of funds. This results in the commonly observed decline in gold prices in the initial phase of a major broad market downturn.

Where gold truly shines is in its ability to recover from that initial weakness, following a market shock, and go on to outperform other assets.

Gold During Recent Major Market Downturns

Examining gold’s performance during past periods of economic trouble helps to better see how gold performs in these situations.

The global financial crisis of 2008, which was a balance-sheet-driven liquidation event, saw correlations across assets – equities, commodities, credit, and real estate – converge toward one as investors were forced to unwind their leveraged positions and market liquidity evaporated.

During 2008, global equities were off 55%, broad commodity indices were off 36%, high yield credit was off around 26%, meanwhile, gold was up 5.5% on a full-year basis.

While gold initially sold off alongside other assets, because of its high liquidity characteristic explained above, it recovered earlier than equities and ended 2008 with positive returns. Portfolios which contained at least some gold, experienced shallower peak-to-trough losses.

Volatility spiked across assets, including gold. However, gold’s relatively limited and temporary drawdown, combined with its rapid recovery, materially reduced damage to portfolios.

Then in 2011 came the Eurozone sovereign debt crisis and a downgrade of U.S. sovereign debt. These events could be described as confidence shocks. There was a sharp uptick in credibility risk tied to both government and private institutions, with concerns surrounding Eurozone sovereign solvency rising, the S&P downgrading U.S. sovereign debt, and persistent fears of a banking system collapse.

During this year, global equities declined roughly 25% peak-to-trough, with the financial sector equities performing particularly badly. Gold, meanwhile, rose by 10% in 2011, which followed a 30% increase in 2010. While gold put in a strong performance in 2011, it was not without periods of sharp pullbacks. But the overall positive direction of gold and the resultant negative correlation to equities helped to offset equity losses in investor portfolios.

The next hard and somewhat sudden hit to the global economy came in 2020, when the pandemic froze activity. The COVID crisis was the fastest equity drawdown in modern history driven by a sudden stop to global activity, forced deleveraging, and extreme uncertainty initially around policy response.

Governments and monetary authorities were quicker to respond and more generous in their loosening of policy than they were during the financial crisis of 2008. This shortened the pain period in markets. Nonetheless, in the February-March 2020 period, global equities were off around 34% in a month, credit markets were in a state of severe dysfunction, and oil prices fell into negative territory. During this period, gold also experienced a decline, but was quick to recover and by the middle of 2020 was at new record high levels. Gold again experienced that all-too-familiar initial liquidation, but its decline was shallower and recovery was faster than equities. Gold went on to reach a record high by the middle of 2020 and also outperformed most defensive assets over the course of the full year.

A combination of strong policy response during the pandemic coupled with supply chain disruptions post the pandemic resulted in an inflation surge during 2022. Inflation rose to levels not seen in decades and caused monetary policy to shift from a loose stance to one of tightening. This caused stocks and bonds to decline simultaneously. Gold did not have a particularly strong year either, ending the year flat. But while gold did not have positive returns, it did not compound losses and helped reduce the overall portfolio drawdown when stocks and bonds both declined simultaneously and real rates rose. The key takeaway regarding gold’s performance during 2022 was that gold does not need a strong positive return during a crisis but needs to be relatively resilient compared to other assets to limit the portfolio's drawdown.

In each of these episodes, gold’s volatility picked up, but the metal hedged various risks across very different crisis types. The global financial crisis of 2008 was a liquidity collapse. The 2011 European sovereign debt crisis and U.S. sovereign debt downgrade were confidence shocks. The pandemic crash resulted from a non-economic, non-financial market shock. And the pullback in markets during 2022 was an inflationary shock.

In a world characterized by elevated political, macroeconomic, and financial market risks, gold plays an important role in diversifying portfolios to protect against major drawdowns.

Some allocation to gold is recommended in every portfolio. Studies have shown that an up to 25% allocation to gold can yield some of the best risk-reward outcomes, but even a more modest allocation of 5% to 10% reduces peak-to-trough portfolio losses.

The reduction in portfolio drawdowns also has a positive impact on investor behavioral outcomes during periods of market stress. It is observed that portfolio drawdowns exceeding 20% often increase the likelihood of investors exiting the markets at the worst possible times. Shallower drawdowns reduce emotional decision-making improving the portfolio’s performance over the longer term.

data.title: With A 7% Dividend Yield And Growth Strategy, Verizon's Outlook Takes Center Stage
data.description:
Intro

Lately, I’ve been analyzing quite a few high‑yield dividend stocks, and two trends have really stood out to me. First, although it’s a broad generalization, many of these stocks have been trending higher recently. Second, it has become surprisingly difficult to find high‑yielders whose prospects actually suggest that the yield is sustainable.

The first issue might be tied to the belief that a change in the Fed chair could lead to a shift toward lower interest rates. This means fixed-income securities will also see lower yields, making dividends seem more valuable and increasing the appeal of high-yield stocks.

To break down the second issue, I think it's a good idea to look at the process of how I evaluate so-called dividend stocks. I’ll explain briefly.

When I look at a high-yielding company for the first time, my process always begins with opening the cash flow statement. I want to see whether the company’s dividend payments are reasonably covered by free cash flow. If they aren’t, I usually stop right there. With only rare exceptions, I’ve found that a persistent mismatch between cash flow and dividend payouts almost always results in a dividend cut sooner or later.

If the cash flow looks healthy, the next thing I examine is the company’s debt load — particularly the net‑debt‑to‑EBITDA ratio. This metric often serves as a warning signal for creditors, who ultimately have significant influence over whether a company can continue paying dividends. When leverage is high, creditors may pressure management to reduce or suspend dividends to protect the balance sheet and their own interests. These restrictions are defined in the company’s credit agreements, which include specific leverage thresholds that trigger consequences if the company breaches them.

This brings me back to Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) – first, it caught my eye as one of the few well-known large-cap companies, if I exclude REITs and MLPs, currently offering a dividend yield above 7%. Secondly, printing free cash flow at an impressive rate – last quarter it came in at roughly $7 billion. On a 12-month trailing basis, it's just over $20 billion. Outstanding figures, no doubt. Next, debt on the balance sheet — well… a hair under $150 billion. That’s even more massive in scale than cash flow.

Although such a mountain of debt may look alarming, I think it's better to be viewed in context. If we follow the simplest logic without diving into the net debt/EBITDA ratio — which can feel a bit like rocket science for investors who haven’t studied economics or just aren’t comfortable with such multiples—a back-of-the-envelope calculation says that if you earn $20 and owe $150, it would take 7.5 years to pay it back, right?

To sum up, in Verizon’s case, while the massive debt load is certainly staggering, the equally impressive free cash flow keeps the company fairly well balanced overall, and at first glance, the dividend doesn’t appear to be in any immediate danger as I see it.

That was the thinking behind taking a closer look at Verizon — to find out whether it’s truly a last‑man‑standing story among high‑yield quality megacaps, or whether something beneath the surface isn’t immediately obvious. With Verizon’s earnings set to be announced on January 30, I’m also looking into what to expect on that front.

In addition, if my approach to evaluating high-yield dividend stocks resonates, I’ve conducted several analyses of names with dividend yields above 7% that appear to have sustainable payouts and, in my view, a strong likelihood of continuing. These include Conagra Brands (CAG), Western Union (WU), Western Midstream (WES), and the MLP pure play Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). Let’s see if VZ could be added to that list.

Why has the stock been underperforming

When I look at what has happened with Verizon lately, it seems fairly clear why the stock has been weak. Investors aren’t satisfied with the company’s growth prospects and, some might say, have lost faith in it. On top of that, there’s been a CEO change to reset the growth narrative: after seven years with the company, Hans Vestberg is out, and PayPal veteran David Schulman is in.

Often, such a move might spark a rally on hopes that a new CEO can reignite growth. In this time, however, the transition seemed to bring even more uncertainty — It wasn’t the official reason, of course, but there was speculation that Schulman was pushed out of PayPal (PYPL) after the stock tanked while other tech companies' shares rallied.

On Verizon’s one-year chart, I highlighted in yellow the period last October when the stock dropped after the CEO change.

The stock started climbing after Verizon’s latest quarterly results, as David Schulman outlined the company’s new strategic initiatives on the earnings call, which seemed to restore some market confidence. However, it has since fallen back, putting us roughly where we were after news of his appointment.

I think it shows the market clearly needs some more proof that the new CEO can truly make a difference, which makes the next quarterly call especially crucial. In my view, the outlook for 2026 will matter far more than the trailing Q4 results.

Why is the market skeptical about the new CEO?

Let’s take a look at PayPal’s stock chart during Schulman’s tenure from 2015 to 2023, noting that he officially stepped down on December 31, 2023. His departure is highlighted in yellow:

From what I’ve read, most of the backlash stems from the fact that he wasn’t able to get the stock moving higher after PayPal briefly touched its all‑time high of around $310 in July 2021, only to plunge to about $60 by the time he left in 2023. Since then, PayPal’s share price has stayed roughly at the same levels up to today, continuing to underperform compared to the S&P 500 (SP500).

However, if we take a closer look at PayPal’s operational performance during Schulman’s tenure, he actually managed to roughly triple the company’s revenue and successfully guided PayPal to stand on its own and grow after the spinoff from eBay (EBAY) in 2015.

PayPal remains a successful leader in its category to this day, although the growth numbers it reported until 2021 under the helm of Schulman haven't yet rebounded. In any case, this doesn’t seem like a failure but rather the opposite in terms of company performance — the stock just followed a different trajectory, and I believe the market had baked in overly ambitious growth expectations when PayPal stock hit its all‑time highs during the pandemic’s high‑growth phase.

In addition, Schulman is already a Verizon veteran, having served on the Verizon Board since 2018. In my view, this can be seen as a major positive, as he doesn’t need a long adjustment period, and any strategic shift could therefore happen more quickly.

I would sum up this section by saying that I partly understand the market’s skepticism — Schulman wasn’t able to convince investors of PayPal’s renewed growth story after the stock’s severe decline. However, this shouldn’t be taken to mean that he would be unable to turn things around at Verizon, given that he managed to triple PayPal’s revenue and maintain strong profitability throughout his tenure.

First hints about Verizon's new growth strategy

The newly appointed CEO shared Verizon’s new strategic approach during the latest earnings call, highlighting his perspective on where growth could come from in a highly saturated market, with an estimated 98% of eligible people already using mobile phones:

Depends on how you count it, somewhere between 5 million and 8 million new postpaid subs that enter into the industry every year. There's obviously switcher pools that are available. There's new and growing services, whether that be convergence or AI-powered infrastructure that can come in. So there's a lot of opportunity in the industry.

The challenges of attracting new customers were highlighted:

..today, we take somewhere between 0% and some very low percent of the new-to-industry postpaids we're clearly losing share..

He also pointed out the shortcomings in his view of the previous strategy:

For the past few years, our financial growth has relied too heavily on price increases, a strategic approach that relies too much on price without subscriber growth is not a sustainable strategy.

The shift he believes is necessary:

The first pillar is about shifting from being a technology-centric to being a customer-centric company. This is about delighting our customers. This is about growing through retention basically.

Looking ahead to the upcoming earnings results

Last quarter, the company delivered adjusted earnings of $1.21 per share, slightly above the analyst consensus of $1.19. Total revenue rose 1.5% to $33.8B, missing expectations by nearly $500 million. Wireless service revenue came in at $21B, up 2.1% YOY, while wireless equipment revenue reached $5.6B, up 5.2%. Both figures were slightly below expectations.

Highlights from Q3 operations:

Outlook

The outlook for the rest of 2025 remained unchanged:

Although the company kept its outlook unchanged, analysts have taken a more cautious view. Two analysts have raised their revenue estimates, while ten have revised them downward. Regarding EPS, only one analyst moved estimates higher, compared with eleven who lowered theirs.

I think it’s fair to conclude that we’re heading into earnings with a considerable degree of caution, which in my view increases the likelihood of a positive stock reaction afterward.

Dividend safety

Since the Verizon dividend at its current level is clearly one of the key reasons for owning the stock, it’s important to take a closer look at the different perspectives on it.

The company does a good job of clarifying its ability to sustain the dividend. The presentation shows that everything looks solid on that front: free cash flow is almost twice the dividend, and the net‑debt‑to‑EBITDA ratio is also comfortably within the required range.

Given AT&T’s (T) dividend cut, I think the market has clearly been pricing in that risk for Verizon, especially since the new CEO could take a different view on the previous dividend strategy. That said, David Schulman was crystal clear, reiterating multiple times his commitment to maintaining the existing dividend approach and prioritizing shareholder returns.

Notable points from the latest earnings conference call related to this:

In September, we raised the dividend for the 19th consecutive year reflecting our continued commitment to shareholder returns.

I expect the actions we are taking will enable us to generate higher free cash flow in 2026 and 2025, even when we include Frontier. Our business is generating strong cash flow today, but I believe it can be even stronger. I am committed to prioritizing the customer experience, while maximizing returns for our shareholders

This includes an ironclad commitment to our dividend, continued debt repayment and value-creating capital return.

We'll continue to be steadfastly committed to our dividend. And we will review, frankly, other opportunities to return capital to shareholders.

..the dividend is sacrosanct to us. We are going to examine every element of our OpEx and our CapEx, and we are going to fully fund all of our initiatives with OpEx savings and drop them to the bottom line so that we can accelerate EPS growth as well.

I think the key takeaway here is that he is strongly committed to maintaining the current dividend strategy and, more broadly, clearly intends to drive higher profitability at Verizon. The recent announcement of 13,000 job cuts is strong evidence of that.

Valuation

Given Verizon’s high debt level, I think it's appropriate to value the company by focusing on its EV/EBITDA multiple, which currently stands at 6.67. This compares with a sector average of 8.33 and Verizon’s own five‑year average of 7.35, both of which point toward undervaluation. Looking at peers leads to the same conclusion: AT&T (T) trades at around 7x, while T‑Mobile (TMUS) is closer to 9.5x, suggesting Verizon remains relatively undervalued.

I rate VZ as a Buy based on these assumptions and my belief that the market underestimates the new CEO’s ability to reignite the company’s growth prospects.

Risks

I think the biggest risk for Verizon with the new CEO appointment right now is execution. It’s definitely a really challenging task to change strategy at a company the size of Verizon. I don’t see any single risk that clearly stands out above the others. All of these factors — competitiveness in acquiring new customers, successfully deploying investments, managing high debt load, and navigating the emerging AI landscape, among many others — in my view ultimately come down to execution.

Takeaway

I think the market will closely evaluate the new CEO’s strategic insights and his outlook for 2026 during the next quarterly earnings release. I believe it won’t be an easy task for him to gain the market’s support, but I remain optimistic and see no obvious reason to doubt his ability to lead Verizon successfully and improve its performance.

That said, patience is clearly required to see the full impact of his efforts, as these are not one or two‑quarter fixes. In my view, the market may currently be pricing Verizon’s prospects too conservatively, which supports my Buy rating.

data.title: IJJ: Mid-Cap Value Is The Wrong "Value" Now
data.description:
The iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF (IJJ), launched on 07/24/2000 by BlackRock Inc. and managed by BlackRock Fund Advisors, provides exposure to the mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity market with a value bias. It charges an expense ratio of 0.18% and manages $8.4 billion, as of January 20, 2026.

The ETF has an interesting approach to mid-cap exposure that is worth considering and the fees are fair for what it provides. However, there are cheaper options available for more or less the same level of exposure and near-term risks point to large-cap value being the more balanced way to manage valuation risk, which is intensely present in the current market structure.

Methodology

The S&P MidCap 400 Value Index starts with the S&P MidCap 400 as its available universe to build its portfolio. It uses classic metrics to define value, such as Book Value to Price, Earnings yield, and Sales to Price. Each stock is scored according to these metrics and 33% of the most relatively undervalued stocks are defined as value, with the rest categorized into growth and blended baskets.

The constituents are float-adjusted market-cap-weighted, but the methodology is nuanced so as to include some stocks that are in the blended category as well. Basically, 100% of the market capitalization of a value stock is taken into account during weighting, but a fraction of the market cap of a blended-style stock is used to determine its weight in the index. That is an interesting touch, considering the more rigid approach of so many other value indices.

Rebalancing is done annually, specifically on the third Friday of December.

Portfolio

By looking at the top holdings, it's clear that concentration, and therefore idiosyncratic risk, is low since the biggest name has a 1.1% weight and the top ten stocks account for just under 10% of the fund. Further, it's natural for a mid-cap value fund to be represented by "old economy" names. Alcoa (AA), Reliance (RS), and WESCO (WCC) provide exposure to cyclical drivers and US Foods (USFD) and Performance Food Group Company (PFGC) are sensitive to restaurant/hospitality cycles. Moreover, names like Annaly (NLY) and Pinnacle Financial (PNFP) are signaling high interest rate sensitivity.

This is certainly not the defensive kind of a value portfolio, therefore. The sector breakdown confirms this because rate and credit sensitivity are huge here (Financials at ~24% and Real Estate at ~10%). Industrials exposure at ~18% and Consumer Cyclical at ~14% also imply a pro-cyclical portfolio. Consequently, the more defensive sectors, such as healthcare and Staples, have modest allocations. However, tech exposure is also very low at ~9%, which implies meaningful divergence from the broader market in bull runs and bear markets.

Alternatives

For investors looking to track the same index but for lower fees, the Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF (IVOV) is the only alternative, charging an expense ratio of 0.10%.

If the index is not a problem and what matters is mid-cap value exposure as cheaply as possible, then the Vanguard Mid-Cap Value ETF (VOE), with an expense ratio of 0.07%, seems like the next best fit. Its portfolio is also less cyclically exposed, with Financials and Industrials representing a smaller part of the pie and Utilities and Tech accounting for about 11% each.

Of course, if fees don't need to be absolutely low but there is a need for a more aggressive approach, then the Invesco S&P MidCap 400 Pure Value ETF (RFV) can offer that. For an expense ratio of 0.35%, it provides higher Consumer Cyclical and Tech exposure (25.5% and 12.6%, respectively). The index it tracks (S&P MidCap 400 Pure Value Index) starts with the same parent, the S&P MidCap 400, and ends up with about 100 stocks that are considered "deep value". A big difference here is the weighting approach; it's factor-weighted instead of market-cap-weighted. Another important detail is that this index doesn't blend styles like the S&P MidCap 400 Value Index.

Performance

The performance differentials between IJJ and IVOV haven't widened significantly over the long run, as expected. However, in recent years, RFV with its pure value approach has pulled ahead as it's more regime-sensitive.

Interestingly, RFV didn't experience a larger drawdown back in 2022, which makes its performance in the last 5 years even more remarkable for a value fund.

Regardless, long-term investors should remember that RFV lagged IJJ for the largest part of the previous decade, which puts into context how valuable its approach is relative to the high expense ratio.

Risks

The soft-landing scenario, which appears more and more likely with persisting easing and more modest headwinds, implies unemployment stabilizing, nonfarm payroll growth recovering, and inflation moderating (the last has already started happening). This presents an opportunity risk for IJJ as long-duration assets, mostly represented by broad-market and growth ETFs, are likely to keep outperforming.

At the same time, growth has recovered since around a year ago and forecasts don't indicate a contraction in the near term. However, they do point to below-average growth for some time. This implies a tailwind for tech-heavy portfolios as they are less sensitive to cyclical drivers that should punish mid-cap value stocks, found in cyclical sectors as we saw.

Conclusion

IJJ is a cheap option for mid-cap value exposure but not the cheapest. Moreover, RFV provides a more aggressive approach that has been working in the last couple of years without penalizing investors with more extreme drawdowns in risk-off periods. Last, mid-cap value as a broader option seems like a poor alternative to broad equity exposure because of the mid-cap constraint. Large-cap value enjoys some perks of the mega caps' tailwinds, while still hedging for valuation risk. I am rating IJJ a hold.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: What CEOs Had To Say At Davos
data.description:
There is no shortage of takes on the political speeches at Davos for the World Economic Forum, 2026. I'll be skipping those here; I've already covered Greenland and the tariff issue; you can read that here.

Instead, I wanted to cover what some of the business leaders who spoke at Davos had to say and what we can take away from what they had to say; these are, namely, Microsoft's (MSFT) Satya Nadella and JPMorgan's (JPM) Jamie Dimon.

Nadella Argues There is No AI Bubble

You can watch the conversation here.

The central theme of the conversation with Larry Fink of BlackRock (BLK) and Satya Nadella was focused around AI and specifically the diffusion of AI, not only agents operating in the economy but also infrastructure like data centers (which he calls "token factories" in his metaphor) and electrical grid expansion.

Nadella tries to lay out the case that there is no AI bubble, largely because he believes that there is existing--and that there will be future--demand for the supply of AI capacity being created. He explains that one of the ways that they will ensure demand for AI is that they are diffusing the infrastructure and that Microsoft is spending 50% of their hyperscaler budget on ex-U.S. AI infrastructure to this end. Specifically here, he evokes the Global South and how building data centers in ex-U.S. geographies is important for the overall diffusion of AI services and how Microsoft will make money moving forward.

And it's true, of the 8,000+ data centers currently operating around the world, over 7,000 are in suboptimal climates, built not because of efficiency, but because of national sovereignty. It makes sense to build a data center in a place that's too hot for optimal data center operations if your whole country is too hot, and you need data centers in your country. Mandela discusses how critical sovereignty is in his forum and how countries around the world are going to continue to want and fight for data sovereignty from the model providers and hyperscalers.

Here's a map of those 8,000 data centers and global temperatures for reference. Most of them are still in the U.S. and Europe.

Nadella argues that this is ultimately a good thing because the diffusion of AI will enable more people across the world to use it and to create economic benefits with it, a central outcome necessary for the AI build-out to not be a bubble. He calls the charges about GDP growth being led by AI capex as being a "point in time" argument and says that it is still too early to see AI productivity in the economic data.

One of the central points that Nadella makes and attempts to lay out for the audience is that token costs, which largely drive the cost of operating AI models, are falling. As we expand the energy grid and expand our supply of AI capacity, token prices will continue to fall. This oversupply of tokens and the build-out of energy grid capacity to generate said tokens have already been driving the price down. The price of a million tokens halved in the last year.

Nadella's argument finishes with the point that I believe most investors would consider actionable: he is bullish on emerging markets and small firms. Nadella says that this diffusion of AI and the driving down of costs to use and to deploy AI systems means that they will become such a commodity that the most AI-enabled and highest adoption firms will dominate future markets.

He extends this to emerging economies because they often have lower regulatory barriers and can adapt to the incoming technology faster; he says that this time, unlike in other tech buildouts, emerging markets are getting access to AI tools at the same time as developed markets.

At the same time, small firms stand to gain against large firms since they are able to be more flexible and integrate AI tools with less friction. Nadella said that new workflows will benefit small players first, as they're more easily able to integrate them, and that there may be a new class of firms coming out of this AI build-out.

I'm not sure that I buy that last one; I still don't think that we're in for a great rotation. But the idea is that if the AI build-out is not truly a bubble, then the beneficiaries will be spread out across markets, and that is a charge for portfolio diversity. Nadella is not predicting that Microsoft and the other hyperscalers will eat the world -- he's predicting the opposite. Emerging market and small-cap exposure may be preferable to large caps if he's right, as they have a lot of catching up to do valuation-wise.

Dimon Was Much More Hesitant

You can watch the conversation here.

The first thing Jamie Dimon discussed was how they're implementing AI at JPM and how he believes that it's transformational in the workforce because of its ability to be applied to basically every facet of his business as a non-tech business. This leans on a point Nadella brought up earlier in the day that one of the hallmarks of the AI build-out not being a bubble is the rate at which AI is being applied to non-tech businesses. Dimon confirming it at JPM is a good sign for the AI bulls.

What wasn't a good sign for the AI bulls was how much hedging Dimon did on what the future will look like; he mentioned how many jobs are likely to be lost and that the government will likely need to step in and help people with retraining or early retirements, something that historically has not gone very well in past iterations. His great fear here was that AI progress is moving too fast and that a displaced workforce will not be able to adapt fast enough and that the economy will not be nimble enough to absorb them elsewhere.

When asked about how AI is impacting the workforce at J.P. Morgan itself, he mentioned that it's unlikely for there to be mass layoffs, but that he does expect the JPM workforce to shrink. Dimon didn't explicitly mention the "boiling frog" metaphor, but he did make the note of, "We would do [layoffs] slowly."

For reference, JPM is at its all-time high total employee count, and Dimon may have signaled that it just peaked.

The second half of the discussion went over economic policies.

Specifically, for the financial sector, Dimon said that President Trump's 10% credit card cap command would be disastrous for the economy (and therefore his firm, which is a huge credit card issuer via Chase). He said it would remove credit access from up to 80% of Americans, who would no longer be eligible if the highest interest rate was capped at 10%. I believe these figures because they likely come from JPM's internals having calculated the effects of this. He was very confident in his assertions that this would be terrible for JPM and financials at large.

He said that not only would it hurt his firm as the issuer, but also the real economy: retailers, restaurants, municipalities, etc., because much of consumer spending is currently being done on credit. That spending loss would be economically disastrous.

The most actionable piece of Dimon's talk at Davos was this: Financials are currently experiencing overreach from the administration and could have a large negative catalyst in front of them if Congress does codify or Trump pushes an executive order to limit credit card rates.

This makes me hesitant to overweight financial companies or sectors, although it may depend on the particular company you're considering. For example, my colleague Julia Ostian made a very good argument for why this news shouldn't affect one's view of American Express (AXP).

Conclusion

There is a lot of bluster at Davos, and many of the conversations and speeches I listened to were largely political in nature or circled back to the same topic that I think Nadella covered best: how AI will shape the economy.

Dimon introduced some hesitancy and a little more realism into the conversation, which I think is necessary. But largely, the tone was bullish on AI overall and the current trajectory of mega caps in the economy, which drive the S&P 500.

There's a lot of optimism from the voices I have heard at Davos about AI and its impacts on the economy, and I think it overshadows the hesitancy and doom that I heard. That may just be financial elitist techno-optimism on display, but it also seems grounded in some reality -- one that could play out in front of us. It seems current optimism largely favors emerging markets and small caps, while disfavoring financial companies and those sectors with deep regulatory environments.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: SPMO: What Happens When Momentum Wanes
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) is one of my favorite funds. Its momentum strategy consistently outperforms the S&P 500 (SPY), and it does so without over-reliance on the Mag 7 stocks or the technology sector.

However, momentum has recently waned in the broader market. The SPY is trading at the same prices it traded at back in October, and SPMO has made no progress since early August. Furthermore, rotations have generally been out of strong-performing stocks and into value. This article looks at what could happen to SPMO if this situation continues.

SPMO Basics

SPMO was launched in 2015. While it has outperformed SPY since inception, it only really distanced itself in 2024 and 2025.

The recent growth hasn't gone unnoticed, and the fund's AUM has ballooned in recent years.

SPMO tracks the S&P 500 Momentum Index, which is designed to capture stocks exhibiting strong price momentum. This momentum can be sustained in certain environments and lead to huge moves. SPMO will catch the likes of Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR) when they are making significant rallies.

SPMO Methodology

SPMO is reconstituted twice a year, in March and September. It holds around 100 stocks, and these can be changed quite considerably. The last reconstitution in September added 41 stocks and deleted 40 stocks, while also changing the weights of retained stocks. This led to a very different top 10 and sector exposure, with technology increasing to a 34% weighting.

I sometimes have my doubts about the additions and deletions. The methodology primarily rewards a large 12-month price change and therefore adds stocks that can look stretched. But either you believe in momentum—and its tendency to persist—or you don't.

Here is a deeper look at the exact methodology:

First, the momentum value is calculated. This is based on the 12-month price change (excluding the most recent month).

The momentum value is then adjusted for volatility. Lower volatility in uptrends is rewarded, as is higher volatility in downtrends.

A winsorized Z-score is calculated to standardize the values. The stocks with the top 100 scores are selected.

Weights are calculated by market cap combined with momentum score, with a 9% cap.

This is the current top 10 and sector exposure:

Meta (META) has performed poorly since the last rebalancing. And Oracle (ORCL) was a poorly timed addition. But that is the risk of the strategy, and for every ORCL that reverses lower, there could be an NVDA that keeps trending higher.

Consequences of Low Momentum

Short-term momentum was extremely high in mid-2025 but has cooled off since late July. SPMO traded below the August peak last week and has generally moved sideways for around 4 months.

It has underperformed SPY since the end of October.

Of course, this could be a much-needed rest after a huge rally. The issue is what could happen to SPMO's portfolio if broader market momentum stays low.

If stocks were to generally tread water until March, SPMO would still have some valuable data to work with, as it uses the price changes over the previous 12 months (excluding the most recent month). There have been some significant moves from the April '25 lows that will be considered in its calculations.

Any stocks that have returned near to the April lows will likely be cut. That could mean META, ORCL, and Netflix (NFLX) will be replaced. Alphabet (GOOGL) could be a big addition.

It would get really interesting if 2026 is flattish into September, as that would mean the calculations will be based on low 12-month price changes. Midterm years do tend to dip over Q2 and Q3, and technology stocks in the Nasdaq (QQQ) can be generally weaker.

That could lead to higher momentum scores in recently rotationally favored stocks and sectors and lead to major changes in SPMO's portfolio. But what if that's just when tech and the usual leaders take off again? SPMO would not adjust for another 6 months.

This is all speculation, of course, but I like to think ahead. SPMO does have flaws, and the way it can completely change nearly half of its holdings based on the previous 12-month price changes means it could adjust just when the market is changing again.

It's not that SPMO will suddenly become a "Sell." Indeed, SPMO has been very good at adapting to various market conditions, and if tech underperforms for any length of time, the reconstitutions will be beneficial. Furthermore, it tends to hold up well during drawdowns. However, it can underperform, and not every year will be like 2024 or 2025.

Conclusions

Momentum in the S&P 500 is stalling. It may be temporary, but if it continues, it could lead to some major changes in SPMO's holdings and a flatter performance in 2026. My concern is that it could make major portfolio changes at the wrong times based on the previous 12-month's data. What if it added ORCL and increased Broadcom's (AVGO) weighting right at the highs? And what if it adjusts to a value/defensive tilt just when the market shifts back towards growth/tech again?

That's only speculation at this point and isn't likely to happen until the September rebalancing. For now, SPMO remains a key part of my ETF holdings.
